high_flag_rate.text: Hi {flw_name}, I noticed some of your recent visits were flagged for review. I wanted to check in — are you having any difficulty with the MUAC measurement process?
Yes, sometimes the children are moving a lot and it's hard to get an accurate reading.

That's very common! A few tips: try measuring when the child is calm, perhaps while the caregiver holds them. Also make sure the tape is snug but not tight on the left arm, midway between the shoulder and elbow.
OK, I will try that. Should I re-measure if the child moves?
Yes, always re-measure if you're unsure. It's better to take an extra minute than to record an inaccurate reading. Your supervisor can also observe a few measurements to give you feedback.

missing_visits.text: Hi {flw_name}, I see you had fewer visits than expected last week. Is everything alright? Were there any barriers to completing your rounds?
There was heavy rain for two days and I could not reach some households.
I understand — weather can be a real challenge. When that happens, try to prioritize households with children who had concerning MUAC readings previously. They need follow-up most urgently.
Good idea. I will plan my route to visit the high-priority ones first.
Great plan. Also, please log the missed visits in the app so your supervisor knows the reason — it helps with scheduling catch-up visits.

low_approval_rate.text: Hi {flw_name}, I wanted to follow up on your recent submission quality. Some of your visits had incomplete data. Can we talk about what fields might be getting missed?
Sometimes I forget to fill in the consent question before doing the MUAC reading.

That's an important one — the consent field is required before the MUAC measurement section unlocks. Try going through the form sections in order without skipping ahead. The app is designed to guide you through the right sequence.
I will be more careful to follow the form order.
Perfect. If you have any trouble with the form flow, reach out to your supervisor — they can walk through a practice submission with you.

new_hire_checkin.text: Welcome {flw_name}! How is your first week going? I wanted to check if you have any questions about the MUAC screening process.
It's going well but I'm not sure what to do when a child has a red MUAC reading.
Great question! A red reading (below 11.5 cm) indicates Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM). You should immediately refer the child to the nearest health facility and record the referral in the app.

Yellow (11.5-12.5 cm) is Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM). Also refer to a health facility, but it's less urgent. Make sure to follow up on the next visit to see if the child received care. You're doing great — keep asking questions!
Thank you, I feel more confident now.

positive_reinforcement.text: Hi {flw_name}, I wanted to share some great news — your approval rate this month is one of the highest in the program! Your data quality has been excellent.
Thank you! I have been trying to be very careful with the measurements.
It shows! Your MUAC measurements are consistent and your referral documentation is thorough. The program coordinator specifically mentioned your work as an example for other health workers.
That makes me happy. I want to help the children in my community.
Your dedication is making a real difference. Keep up the excellent work, and don't hesitate to reach out if you ever need support.

coaching_resolved_clean.text: Hi {flw_name}, your supervisor flagged a small data-quality issue from this week's visits. Got a minute to talk it through?
Yes, sure. Was it the photo angle issue?
That's exactly it — a few of your MUAC photos were framed too tight to see the measurement window. The audit photos all passed though, so this is just a small refresher.
I think I know which ones. I was rushing because one of the houses had a sick child waiting.
That's completely understandable. Two quick tips: keep the camera ~30 cm back so the colour bands are visible, and re-take if the reticle isn't fully in frame. Want to look at one of yours together?

When you open yesterday's batch in the app, look at the third photo — the reading window is partly covered by the thumb. Slightly rotating the wrist before you take the shot fixes that. Try a few practice shots next visit and your supervisor will spot-check.
Got it, I'll be careful with framing. Thanks for the help.
Anytime. Closing this task as resolved — you're doing great work overall, {flw_name}.

coaching_formal_warning.text: Hi {flw_name}. The audit on your recent visits found a pattern we need to address — multiple photos in a row had measurement issues. This needs to be a formal coaching call.
Okay. I wasn't sure all of them were a problem.

I'll walk through what we found. Of your last five MUAC photos, three were unreadable due to framing and two looked like the tape was applied loosely. That kind of clustering tells us the issue isn't an off day — it's a habit that needs to change.
I see. I've been doing the visits very quickly because there are so many in my area.
I understand the pressure. But these measurements drive referrals for severe acute malnutrition — if we miss them, real children miss treatment. Speed matters less than accuracy here.
You're right. I will slow down.

Good. Three concrete things for this week: (1) Re-do the MUAC training module before your next visit. (2) Your supervisor will shadow your first three visits next week. (3) I'm recording this as a formal warning. If photo quality drops again next week, the next step is suspension from the active roster.
Understood. I will not let this happen again.
I believe you. Closing this task as 'warned'. Reach out if you need a refresher on any specific measurement step before your next visit, {flw_name}.

coaching_repeat_offense_suspension.text: Hi {flw_name}. We're following up on the audit again this week. I'm sorry to say the issues from your formal warning two weeks ago have continued.
I tried to do better. What did the audit find?

All five of your MUAC photos this week were flagged. The reviewers' conclusion was that the patterns are consistent with rushed measurements — same framing problems we discussed at the warning, plus two photos that appear to be of the same tape position rather than separate measurements.
I see. I have been struggling to keep up.

I appreciate your honesty. Given this is a repeat failure right after a formal warning, the program standard is suspension from the active roster. I want to be clear: this isn't permanent — it's pause to give you time to retrain properly without delivery pressure.

Three things. (1) Effective today you're off the visit roster. (2) Your supervisor will set up a full retraining session for you next week — at a pace that works. (3) After you complete the training and demonstrate competency on practice measurements, you can apply to rejoin.
I understand. I want to come back when I am ready.

That's the right attitude. We'd rather have you out for two weeks and confident than rushing and missing children who need help. Closing this task as 'suspended'. Your supervisor will be in touch about the retraining schedule.

coaching_repeat_offense_fraud_suspension.text: Hi {flw_name}. We need to discuss the audit findings on your recent visits. The reviewers flagged a serious concern.

Multiple photos this week appear to show the MUAC tape on objects that don't look like a child's arm — the reviewer noted the tape was visibly on a finger in one image and on a piece of cloth in another. Combined with your warning two weeks ago, this is a pattern we can't continue with.
I... had trouble reaching some of the households this week.
I hear you. Reaching every household is hard, but submitting photos that aren't real measurements is a line we can't move on. Real children with SAM get missed when measurements aren't taken.

Suspension from the active roster, effective immediately. Your supervisor will reach out about next steps. There's a path back through retraining and a probationary period — but the audit team needs to be confident this won't recur before you'd resume visits.
Closing this task as 'suspended'. I know this is hard news — your supervisor will follow up tomorrow.

coaching_resolved_clean__bad_muac_distri.text: Hi {flw_name}, your supervisor flagged this week's screening numbers — almost none of the children you measured screened as malnourished, which is unusual for your area. Can we talk through which households you visited?
Mostly the compounds near the health post — they are the quickest to reach and I can do more visits in a day.

That explains the pattern. The families closest to the health post tend to be better off — the children most at risk are usually in the farther households. If we only screen the easy ones, we miss the kids who most need referral. Could you plan next week's route to include the outer settlements?
Yes, I can do the far side on Tuesday and Wednesday when I have more time for the walk.
Checking back in — this week's screening profile looks much more like the area's expected picture, including two referrals that got children into treatment. That's exactly the change we hoped for.
The route plan helped. The far households take longer but those visits matter more.
Agreed. Closing this task as resolved — keep that route rotation going, {flw_name}.

coaching_resolved_clean__gender_skew.text: Hi {flw_name}, quick check-in from this week's review — your screening list was almost entirely boys this week, which is outside the balance we'd expect. Is something making it harder to reach the girls?
Some mothers were away at the market with their daughters when I visited in the mornings.

That makes sense. Try splitting your rounds — mornings for some compounds, late afternoon for the ones where you keep missing the girls. Every child under five in the household should be screened, even if it means a second stop.
I will try afternoon visits for those compounds this week.
Checking back — this week's split looks much healthier, roughly half girls. Whatever you changed, it worked.
The afternoon visits helped. I also ask the caregiver to bring every child under five before I start.
That's exactly right. Closing this task as resolved — nice work, {flw_name}.

coaching_formal_warning__bad_muac_distri.text: Hi {flw_name}. The weekly review flagged your screening numbers — across this week's visits, almost no children screened as SAM or MAM. In your catchment that pattern usually means the harder-to-reach households are being skipped. This needs to be a formal coaching call.
The far compounds take most of a day to reach. I have been covering the nearer ones to keep my visit count up.

I appreciate the honesty. But visit counts only matter if they reach the children at risk — and those are concentrated in exactly the households being skipped. Screening only well-fed children near the road isn't coverage, it's the appearance of coverage.
I understand. I can plan two days each week for the outer settlements.

Good. Your supervisor will review next week's route against the household register, and I'm recording this as a formal warning — if the screening profile stays this skewed next week, the next step is suspension from the roster.
I believe you. Closing this task as 'warned', {flw_name} — reach out if you want help planning the route.

coaching_formal_warning__gender_skew.text: Hi {flw_name}. The weekly review flagged your screening list — over 80% of the children you measured this week were boys, well outside the expected balance. This needs to be a formal coaching conversation.
I record whoever the family brings to me when I arrive.

I understand, but the protocol is to screen every child under five in the household, not only the ones brought forward. When girls are missed systematically, malnourished girls go unreferred — that's exactly why this threshold exists.
I see. I did not realize it had become that uneven.

Three things for this week: (1) ask the caregiver to bring every under-five child before you start, (2) record any child you could not screen and why, (3) your supervisor will review next week's split. I'm recording this as a formal warning — if the split stays outside the band, the next step is suspension.
Understood. I will ask for all the children at every visit.
Thank you, {flw_name}. Closing this task as 'warned'. Your supervisor will check in after next week's review.

coaching_formal_warning__misleading_phot.text: Hi {flw_name}. The audit on this week's visits raised a serious concern — several of your MUAC photos don't appear to show a measurement on a child's arm at all. We need to talk about this formally.
Some of the photos were taken quickly. Maybe the angle was bad.

The reviewers considered that — but in two photos the tape appears to be wrapped around something other than an arm. I have to be direct: submitting photos that aren't real measurements is treated as fraud, and a child who isn't measured can't be referred.
I understand. It will not happen again.

For now this is recorded as a formal warning, with re-verification: your supervisor will accompany your next three visits and re-check this week's households. If the audit team sees anything like this again, the program standard is immediate suspension.
Okay. I will redo the visits with my supervisor.
Thank you. Closing this task as 'warned', {flw_name} — please treat this with the seriousness it carries.

coaching_in_progress__bad_muac_distribut.text: Hi {flw_name}, your supervisor asked me to follow up on this week's review. Your screening numbers came in much lower than expected — almost no children flagged as malnourished, which is unusual for your area. Can you walk me through which households you reached this week?
Mostly the ones along the main road. The rains made the paths to the far compounds difficult.

That's understandable — but the far compounds are where most of the at-risk children live, so a week of road-side visits will look exactly like this. Were any of the far households reachable on foot, even if it takes longer?
Some of them, yes. I can try the dry path on the school side and see which ones I can reach.
Please do — map out which of the far households you can reach this week and reply here. Once I have your list, your supervisor and I will decide whether the remaining ones need a re-visit plan.

coaching_in_progress__gender_skew.text: Hi {flw_name}, following up from this week's review — your screening list was heavily skewed toward boys this week. Before anything formal, your supervisor wanted to understand what's happening. Can you talk me through it?
I think it is the timing. The girls are often with their mothers at the market when I come by.
That would explain it. Could you check your visit log — were the girls recorded as absent, or were those households marked fully screened?
I am not sure. I will check the log tonight and tell you.
Thanks — once you've checked, reply here. If the households were marked fully screened with the girls missing, we'll need to schedule re-visits for those compounds.

coaching_in_progress.text: Hi {flw_name}, your supervisor asked me to follow up on a few photos from this week's audit. They want to talk through what happened before flagging anything formally. Have a minute?
Yes. Which photos were the issue?
Two of the MUAC photos from yesterday had readings that didn't look consistent — one read green but the tape position looked too low on the arm. Can you walk me through how you took it?
I think the child was crying and moving so I might have placed the tape too low. I will look at the photo.

Thanks for being honest. Take a look and let me know — if you want to flag it for re-measurement on a follow-up visit, your supervisor can schedule that. Once you've reviewed I'll wait for your reply.
